mcqs.explanation: The continue statement skips the current iteration when i==2, so the print statement is bypassed for i=2. The loop still completes all 5 iterations. Output: 0 1 3 4 (space after 4 optional).

The break only exits the INNER loop. When i=1: j=1 triggers break (count=0). i=2: j=1 increments count (1), j=2 triggers break. i=3: j=1 increments (2), j=2 increments (3), j=3 triggers break. Total count = 6.

mcqs.question: In a Servlet's doGet method, which loop construct is most appropriate to iterate over all parameter names in an HTTP request?
mcqs.explanation: request.getParameterNames() returns an Enumeration<String>. The enhanced for-each loop provides clean iteration. Using while with hasMoreElements() is also valid but less modern. The for(int i=0...) won't work directly as getParameterNames() doesn't return an array.
The labeled break 'break outer' exits BOTH the inner and outer loops when j==1. Only the first iteration (i=0, j=0) executes the print. Output: 00

mcqs.question:
Given: int[] numbers = {10, 20, 30};
Which code correctly uses an enhanced for-each loop to calculate the sum?

options.text: for(int i = 0; i < numbers.length; numbers++) { sum += numbers[i]; }
for(int i in numbers) { sum += i; }
foreach(int num in numbers) { sum += num; }

mcqs.explanation: The enhanced for-each syntax in Java is: for(Type variable : collection/array). Option A incorrectly uses numbers++ (not valid), Option C uses wrong 'in' keyword, Option D uses incorrect 'foreach' keyword.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of searching an element in a sorted array using a linear scan in a loop?
mcqs.explanation: A linear scan visits each element at most once until finding the target. In the worst case (element at last position or not present), it examines all n elements. Time complexity is O(n), also known as linear time.

mcqs.question:
Consider this JSP code: <% for(int i=1; i<=5; i++) { %>
  • Item <%= i %>
    • <% } %> How many list items will be rendered in the output HTML?
mcqs.explanation: The for loop runs from i=1 to i<=5, inclusive. This executes 5 iterations. Each iteration outputs one <li> element. The output HTML will contain 5 list items: Item 1, Item 2, Item 3, Item 4, Item 5.

The while loop prints 0, 1, 2 (stops when i becomes 3). After while completes, i=3. The do-while executes at least once: prints 3 first, then checks 3<3 which is false, so it terminates. Output: 0 1 2 3

mcqs.question: In a Servlet processing multiple checkbox values (e.g., hobbies), which loop is most appropriate?
mcqs.explanation: request.getParameterValues("hobby") returns a String[] of all values for the 'hobby' parameter (multiple checkboxes with same name). Enhanced for-each iterates through this array. Option D is incorrect because the array contains Strings, not ints.
Outer loop i runs 0,1. Inner loop j runs 0,1 for each i. Sequence: i=0,j=0→(0,0); i=0,j=1→(0,1); i=1,j=0→(1,0); i=1,j=1→(1,1). Total 4 coordinate pairs.

mcqs.question: Which statement about infinite loops is TRUE?

options.text: All infinite loops will cause a compile-time error
while(true) is the only way to create an infinite loop
Infinite loops can be terminated using break, return, or System.exit()
for(;;) will always throw an exception eventually

mcqs.explanation: Infinite loops can be created in multiple ways: while(true), for(;;), for(;true;), etc. They can be terminated programmatically using break (exits loop), return (exits method), System.exit() (terminates JVM), or throwing an exception.

mcqs.question: In Java 8+, what will happen when using break inside a stream's forEach()?

options.text: It will compile and break out of the stream processing
It will cause a compile-time error (break cannot be used outside loops)
It will skip the current element and continue

mcqs.explanation: The break statement can only be used within loops (for, while, do-while) or switch statements. Stream's forEach() is a method that takes a lambda/Consumer, not a loop construct. Using break inside forEach() causes compilation error: 'break outside switch or loop'.
Loop starts at i=1 (not 0), so it skips arr[0]=1. Summation: arr[1]+arr[2]+arr[3]+arr[4] = 2+3+4+5 = 14.

mcqs.question: Which loop construct guarantees at least one execution regardless of condition?
mcqs.explanation: The do-while loop checks its condition AFTER executing the loop body. This makes it a post-test loop, guaranteeing at least one execution. Other loops (for, while, enhanced for) are pre-test loops that may execute zero times.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of a triple nested loop where each iterates from 0 to n?
mcqs.explanation: With three nested loops each running n times: total iterations = n × n × n = n³. Therefore, time complexity is O(n³), also known as cubic time. This grows rapidly with input size.
